title: Build A Tiny Pipeline In Bruin Web

intro.subtitle: Create a pipeline from scratch, write the SQL, and watch the graph connect itself
intro.text: Hi I'm Lukas and I created a nice data visualization tool on top of bruin. For now it's called bruin web. You can use it as a notebook tool or as a visual pipeline builder that still allows for version controlling your pipeline definitions.

segments.text: Let's build a tiny pipeline from scratch so you can see the whole flow in one pass. I'll start by creating a fresh pipeline called demo_shop.
First I drop in a customers asset. This one is just a little inline dataset, which is perfect when you want to sketch something out quickly.
Now I'll write the SQL directly in the editor. Nothing fancy here, just a couple of customers so we have something real to build on.

action.content:
select 1 as customer_id, 'Ada' as customer_name
union all
select 2 as customer_id, 'Grace' as customer_name

segments.text: Next up is orders. I'm creating that as a second asset so we have two separate inputs feeding the graph.
I'll keep this one simple too. A couple of orders is enough to make the downstream join obvious when the third asset shows up.

action.content:
select 100 as order_id, 1 as customer_id, 42 as total_amount
union all
select 101 as order_id, 2 as customer_id, 84 as total_amount

segments.text: Now for the interesting part. I'll add one more asset that joins those two inputs together, and Bruin Web should wire the graph automatically once the dependencies are in place.
Here I'm just typing the query itself. The editor stays focused on the SQL, while the rest of the asset details are handled elsewhere in the workspace.

action.content:
select
  orders.order_id,
  customers.customer_name,
  orders.total_amount
from "demo_shop"."orders" as orders
join "demo_shop"."customers" as customers
  on orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id
order by orders.order_id

segments.text: Before inspecting anything, I'll run the pipeline so the upstream pieces are materialized and ready. That gives the joined asset something real to build on.
Now that the pipeline has run, I can inspect the joined asset right here. It's a quick way to sanity-check the result without leaving the workspace.
And if I want to actually run it, I can materialize it from the same spot. Bruin keeps that result history right there while I keep working.
And there it is. The joined model is sitting under the two source assets, and the graph has already drawn those connections for us.
